headline: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act is law — the most consequential federal housing bill in years — while Florida's property-tax ballot measure and a stack of pending financing and supply bills keep the legislative calendar full.
paragraphs: Last week delivered two notable signings. On July 11, President Trump signed HB6644 — the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act — into Public Law 119-101, the broadest expansion of FHA multifamily financing since the 1990s: higher loan limits indexed to inflation, expanded HOME program income eligibility, relief from environmental review for residential construction and rehab, and the end of the permanent-chassis requirement for manufactured homes. Developers and lenders with FHA-backed pipelines should review underwriting assumptions against the new limits now. The day after, Pennsylvania's HB96 (now Act 27 of 2026) took effect, amending the Real Estate Tax Sale Law to let property owners designate a second notice recipient before a tax sale — closing a gap that had cost owners properties they never knew were in jeopardy.

In Florida, the June 16 signing of HJR 1-F (companion H0001) placed constitutional amendments on the ballot: tighter caps on annual assessment increases, a higher homestead exemption, and limits on how counties and municipalities may deploy ad valorem tax revenue. If voters approve, it would reshape property-tax economics for Florida homeowners and affect local-government revenue capacity — a direct concern for developers and lenders modeling NOI on income properties in the state. On the federal floor, the pending LIHTC expansion (HB2725), workforce housing tax credit (HB8626), and office-to-residential conversion credit (HB2410) remain the most actionable items for deal sponsors, as each directly improves the capital stack or feasibility of specific project types; none has cleared both chambers yet.

New York and Pennsylvania continue to generate significant regulatory activity. In New York, the 485-x technical fixes (S10345 and S10500) and the good cause eviction corrections (S08612) are moving through the Assembly, with direct underwriting implications for tax-incentive deals and market-rate multifamily in covered buildings. The Faith-Based Affordable Housing Act (S03397), the Deed Protection Act (A09554), and the office-to-residential conversion tax credit (S09259) remain in committee but represent material opportunities for developers. In Pennsylvania, the anti-predatory-listing-agreement bill (HB986) is in the Senate after passing the House, and the first-time homebuyer savings account bill (HB818) is also in Senate committee — both are further along than most bills in the ledger and worth monitoring for final passage.
